coredata.dc:title: Natural and biocompatible dressing unit based on tea carbon dots modified core-shell electrospun fiber for diabetic wound disinfection and healing
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Zhenyou
Yin, Junhui
Zhou, Xueqing
Li, Suyun
Fu, Zhenyu
Liu, Pei
Shen, Longxiang
Shi, Wenyan
coredata.dc:description: Wound infection and healing in patients with diabetes is one of the complex problems in trauma treatment. Therefore, designing and preparing an advanced dressing membrane for treating the wounds of such patients is essential. In this study, a zein film with biological tea carbon dots (TCDs) and calcium peroxide (CaO2) as the main components for promoting diabetic wound healing was prepared by an electrospinning technique, which combines the advantages of natural degradability and biosafety. CaO2 is a biocompatible material with a microsphere structure that reacts with water to release hydrogen peroxide and calcium ions. TCDs with a small diameter were doped in the membrane to mitigate its properties while improving the antibacterial and healing effects of the membrane. TCDs/CaO2 was mixed with ethyl cellulose-modified zein (ZE) to prepare the dressing membrane. The antibacterial properties, biocompatibility and wound-healing properties of the composite membrane were investigated by antibacterial experiment, cell experiment and a full-thickness skin defect. TCDs/CaO2 @ZE exhibited significant anti-inflammatory and wound healing-promoting properties in diabetic rats, without any cytotoxicity. This study is meaningful in developing a natural and biocompatible dressing membrane for diabetic wound healing, which shows a promising application in wound disinfection and recovery in patients with chronic diseases.

(b) Illustration of the mechanism of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE for promoting chronic wound healing on the full-thickness wound in the STZ-induced diabetic rat model.

Characterization of CaO2, ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (a) SEM analysis and diameter distribution. (b) XRD spectra of CaO2, (c) ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (d) UV–vis–NIR spectrum of CaO2, (e) ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (f) FTIR spectra of zein, ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (g) Thermogravimetric curves of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (h) Water swelling of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (i) Contact angle of zein, ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (j) Curve of in vitro dissolved oxygen content. (k) In vitro drug release curve.

Cytotoxicity assay and antibacterial efficacy of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE in vitro. (a) Viability of L929 cells cultured in ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE extracts for 48 h. (b) Zones of inhibition surrounding the ZE-, TCDs@ZE-, CaO2 @ZE- and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE-infused paper disks against S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli and P. aeruginosa on agar plates. (c) Antibacterial activity of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. (d) Absorbance of the crystal violet-stained biofilms formed by bacteria treated with ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE for 36 h. n = 3 per group. 1–β = 1. (e) Cell viability analysis of bacteria in the presence of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE, NAC or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE + NAC for 3 h by Alamar Blue assay. n = 3 per group. 1–β = 1. (f) Intracellular ROS determined by DCFH-DA staining using a fluorescence microplate reader in S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli and P. aeruginosa treated with ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE, NAC, CaO2 @ZE + NAC or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE + NAC for 3 h. n = 3 per group. 1–β = 1. * p < 0.05, * ** p < 0.001 and ns > 0.05.

TCDs/CaO2 @ZE accelerates skin healing of diabetic wounds. (a) Gross view of diabetic wounds from male Sprague-Dawley rats treated with ZE, TCDs@ZE or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE at the indicated time points. Scale bar, 2 mm. (b) Quantification of the wound healing area. (c) H&E staining of diabetic wounds. (d) Scar width. (e) Masson’s trichrome staining images of diabetic wounds. (f) Quantification of the average intensity for Masson-stained areas. n = 3 per group. 1–β = 1. * p < 0.05 versus the ZE group, #p < 0.05 versus the TCDs@ZE group. * /#p < 0.05, * */##p < 0.01 and * ** /###p < 0.001.

Effect of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE on wound inflammation. (a) QPCR analysis of IL-1β, IL-6 and TNF-ɑ in RAW264.7 macrophages receiving different treatments. n = 3 per group. 1–β = 1. * *p < 0.01 versus the ZE + LPS group, ###p < 0.001 versus the ZE group. * *p < 0.01 and * ** /###p < 0.001.(b) The result of immunofluorescence staining. (c) Quantification of immunofluorescence intensity of CCR7 as the primary antibody, (d) Quantification of immunofluorescence intensity of CD206 as the primary antibody. n = 3 per group. 1–β = 1. 0.001 < **p < 0.01, * **p < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Natural and biocompatible dressing unit based on tea carbon dots modified core-shell electrospun fiber for diabetic wound disinfection and healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound infection and healing in patients with diabetes is one of the complex problems in trauma treatment. Therefore, designing and preparing an advanced dressing membrane for treating the wounds of such patients is essential. In this study, a zein film with biological tea carbon dots (TCDs) and calcium peroxide (CaO2) as the main components for promoting diabetic wound healing was prepared by an electrospinning technique, which combines the advantages of natural degradability and biosafety. CaO2 is a biocompatible material with a microsphere structure that reacts with water to release hydrogen peroxide and calcium ions. TCDs with a small diameter were doped in the membrane to mitigate its properties while improving the antibacterial and healing effects of the membrane. TCDs/CaO2 was mixed with ethyl cellulose-modified zein (ZE) to prepare the dressing membrane. The antibacterial properties, biocompatibility and wound-healing properties of the composite membrane were investigated by antibacterial experiment, cell experiment and a full-thickness skin defect. TCDs/CaO2 @ZE exhibited significant anti-inflammatory and wound healing-promoting properties in diabetic rats, without any cytotoxicity. This study is meaningful in developing a natural and biocompatible dressing membrane for diabetic wound healing, which shows a promising application in wound disinfection and recovery in patients with chronic diseases.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nanofiber Wound dressing Core-shell structure Antibacterial Diabetic

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a disease associated with dysfunctional blood glucose control and disruption of normal metabolism and protein and lipid regulation [1–3]. If insufficiently untreated, diabetes can lead to serious complications, including impaired wound healing, thus resulting in the development of chronic wounds [4]. Persistent chronic trauma severely affects the comfort and well-being of patients and is associated with risks of amputation and even death [3]. Wound sites containing higher levels of glucose are more prone to bacterial infection and exhibit prolonged periods of inflammation, thereby further disrupting the normal repair process [5,6]. Therefore, the prevention of bacterial infection and excessive inflammation remains a major need for diabetic wound repair.

The current status of bacterial resistance in response to antibiotic treatment is highly unfavorable for patients in the long term [7], and new practical antibacterial strategies are needed. To date, many nanomaterials with excellent performance have been reported in the field of wound antibacterial treatment, such as quantum dots [8], metal-organic frameworks [9], metal oxides [10], and biological extracts [11]. A number of feasible tools based on antibiotic-free strategies have been reported, thus verifying the feasibility of multifunctional nanomaterials for collaborative therapy.

CaO2 is a metal peroxide that reacts with H2O under different pH conditions, thereby forming hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) or O2, which is widely used in the biomedical field [12–14]. In addition, Ca2 + , produced by the reaction of CaO2 with water, is an essential signaling molecule in the tissue repair process [15]. Extracellular calcium upregulates the expression of different angiogenesis-related genes, and consequently promotes endothelial cell proliferation and migration [16–18]. However, although a large body of literature has confirmed the biosafety of CaO2 in medical applications, its oxidizing properties must be considered. To mitigate its properties, achieving controlled slow release of CaO2 and binding it to antioxidants are the requirements for CaO2 in wound treatment applications.

Carbon dots (CDs), particularly those obtained by heating natural carbon sources, can inherit properties similar to their derived carbon sources [19–21]. Because of excellent optical properties, modifiable surface and good biocompatibility, CDs are widely used in catalysis, optoelectronic devices, biological imaging, biosensors and other biomedical applications [22,23]. Tea polyphenols have numerous pharmacological properties, including anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidation, anti-aging, angiogenesis promotion, anticancer effects and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, particularly against S. aureus [24–28]. Therefore, tea offers a natural ideal carbon source to synthesize tea carbon dots (TCDs) with good characteristics [29]. Notably, CDs usually require photoinitiation or suitable charge interaction to exert antimicrobial activity [30], thus limiting their application in the practical environment. Therefore, combining carbon dots and other antibacterial agents has become an ideal choice.

Electrospun nanofibers have been widely used in wound dressing research because of their high specific surface area and porosity [31]. The nanofiber structure in the electrospun membrane enables loading of therapeutic drugs to prevent wound infection and promote healing [32]. Because of its biodegradability, cytocompatibility, and good film-forming properties [32–34], zein is an attractive natural material that is widely used in a variety of drug delivery systems, including films, gels, NPs, and nanofibers [35,36]. In addition, because of the hydrophilic balance in its chain structure, it has self-assembly properties and therefore is a good candidate for drug packaging and sustained release systems [37].

In this study, TCD/CaO2 @ZE films were prepared by electrospinning. The solubility characteristics of zein were exploited to self-encapsulate CaO2 nanoparticles (NPs) in the membrane to achieve sustained drug release. Ethylcellulose modified zein was used to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds to improve the mechanical properties and water stability of the membrane·H2O2 and Ca2+ released by CaO2 can play antibacterial and healing roles, respectively, and the doping of TCDs can play a synergistic role to further enhance the therapeutic effect. In vitro experiments and a full-thickness skin defect were used to evaluate the antibacterial properties, cell compatibility and healing effect. As an advanced wound dressing with green raw materials, high performance and comfortable thinness, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE provides a solution to the potential biocompatibility concerns of CaO2 in dressings and displays great potential for application in wound sterilization and healing.

Calcium chloride (99%), H2O2 (30%), ammonia solution (NH4OH, 28–30%), polyethylene glycol 200, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99%) were purchased from Sinopharm. Ethyl alcohol (C2H5OH, ≥99.7%) and ethyl cellulose (6 mPa.s to 9 mPa.s) were obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Zein was purchased from Adamas. The microorganism strains of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis, ATCC 6633), Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa, ATCC 27853) and the components of the Luria-Bertani (LB) medium were purchased from Guangzhou Huankai Biotech Co., Ltd. Alamar Blue Assay Kit, Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) Assay Kit, L-929 fibroblast cells, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Cell Counting Kit-8 were from Shanghai Fanmeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Streptozotocin (STZ) was purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. The staining reagents required for hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E), Masson trichrome staining, primary and secondary antibodies and other reagents for fluorescent staining were provided by Wuhan Servicebio Technology Co., Ltd. Penicillin-streptomycin were obtained from Invitrogen Co., Ltd. Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd.

TCDs [29] and CaO2 [38] were prepared according to the reported method. The precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 400 mg zein in 2 mL TCDs/ethanol solution (v/v=2/8). The required amount of CaO2 (10% w/v) was added to the above mixture and magnetically stirred for 10 min until completely dispersed, ensuring no bubbles were generated. Subsequently, 100 mg ethyl cellulose was added and continuously stirred for 10 min. The parameters used in electrospinning were as follows: 0.60 mm nozzle, applied voltage of 22 kV, flow rate of 0.02 mL·min−1 and distance (from needle tip to collector) of 15 cm. The nanofibers produced were collected on a circular rotating drum covered with aluminum foil. The prepared nanofibers are denoted as TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and ZE were prepared according to the same procedure as above but without adding CaO2 or TCDs or neither, respectively.

The release and reaction rate of CaO2 in the fibrous membrane was assessed by measurement of calcium ion concentrations and dissolved oxygen content at different time points. CaO2 (30 mg), ZE (300 mg), TCDs@ZE (300 mg), CaO2 @ZE (300 mg), and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE (300 mg) were placed in brown 50 mL centrifuge tubes, and 30 mL PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.4) was added to each tube. The tubes were then shaken at 37 °C at 120 rpm, and 3 mL of supernatant was tested at fixed time points (2 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, 72 h, 120 h, 168 h, and 216 h). Subsequently, 3 mL of fresh PBS was re-added. The calcium ion concentration of the extract was determined with a flame atomic absorption spectrophotometer (SP-3803AA). The dissolved oxygen content of the extract was determined with a dissolved oxygen meter (oxi330i).

The antimicrobial activity of the films against S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli and P. aeruginosa was investigated by the agar disk diffusion assay. All experimental instruments used in the antimicrobial activity test were sterilized in an autoclave, and the sterile conditions were maintained throughout the experiment. Briefly, 60 μL bacterial suspensions (1 ×107 colony-forming units (CFU) mL−1) were spread on the LB agar plates. Equal sizes (10 mm in diameter) of the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and ZE membranes (sterilized with UV for 15 min before the experiment) were placed onto the inoculated LB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. The agar plates were photographed and the zones of inhibition around the films were measured.

The bacteriostatic rate test was performed according to our reported method [39]. Different samples (4 ×4 cm2) were immersed in 60 mL microbial suspension at 37 °C and vigorously shaken for 18 h. Subsequently, 5 mL solutions were transferred to a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 1000 r/min. The bacteriostatic rate was finally obtained by calculating the quality difference between the test tube and the blank control. The entire experiment was repeated twice.

All experimental equipment was sterilized prior to use. A total of 2 mL bacterial suspension (OD=0.2) was inoculated on the coverglass in the solid medium and cultured in a 37 °C biochemical incubator for 48 h. The cultured biofilm on the coverglass was washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove planktonic bacteria, crystal violet drops were added to the coverglass for dyeing for 15 min, the excess crystal violet was washed twice with PBS and finally the colored crystal violet was washed with 33% acetic acid. The obtained solution was transferred to a six-well plate, and its absorbance was determined with a microplate reader at 595 nm. All the experiments were performed in triplicate.

The tested bacterial suspensions of 100 μL (5 ×104 CFU mL−1) in 96-well plates were treated with ZE or minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) doses of TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films, N-Acetylcysteine (NAC, 10 mM) or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films + NAC (10 mM) in LB medium containing Alamar Blue (10 μL) for 4 h in the dark. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was determined at 545 nm excitation and 590 nm emission with a microplate reader. Cell viability (%) was evaluated using a standard curve. The tests were performed in triplicate.

A ROS assay kit was used to detect intracellular ROS generation. A total of 5 × 105 CFU per well of activated bacteria was distributed into fluid medium containing ZE or MIC doses of TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films, NAC (10 mM), CaO2 @ZE + NAC (10 mM) or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films + NAC (10 mM) in 24-well plates. All samples were shaken for 1 min to ensure the full release of ROS before being loaded with 10 μM fluorescent probe 2,7-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA). Subsequently, the cultures were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. When the DCFH interacted with ROS, the formation of the fluorescent product DCF was detected by a microplate reader at 485 nm excitation and 535 nm emission [40].

Male Sprague Dawley rats (approximately 200 g, aged 6 weeks) were randomly assigned to three groups for feeding, with three rats in each group. Filtered and sterilized 1% STZ/citric acid buffer (65 mg·kg−1, pH=4.2–4.5) was injected intravenously into the rats. Subsequently, the blood glucose was monitored with blood taken from the tail vein. The model was successful when the venous blood glucose value was 13.9–16.7 mmol/L. Rats that failed to meet the blood glucose standard received further STZ 3 days later. All animal purchases and experimental operations were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine.

After completing the modeling of diabetes, a round full-thickness skin defect with a diameter of 15 mm was removed on the back of SD rats. Following ultraviolet irradiation disinfection, the dressings of the experimental groups and the control group were placed at the skin defect. The healing of the defect model was observed at 0, 3, 6 and 10 days after the operation. The percentage of the healed skin in the original defect area was quantified using Image J software. The rats were euthanized with excessive anesthesia 10 days after the operation, and samples were collected from the wound site and used to prepare paraffin sections. Masson’s trichrome staining and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining were performed to examine the repair effect of antibacterial dressing on the epidermis of diabetes rats.

Immunofluorescence staining of the macrophage markers CCR7 and CD206 was performed to assess the wound inflammatory response. After dewaxing and washing of sections, antigen repair was performed with citric acid (pH=6.0) and microwave heating, and endogenous peroxidase was blocked with 3% hydrogen peroxide. Subsequently, CCR7 (1:100) was added and incubated overnight, secondary antibodies were added and incubated, and the corresponding dye was incubated at room temperature for 10 min. The above steps were repeated for CD206 (1:5000) staining. The nuclei were then counterstained with DAPI, autofluorescence was quenched, images were acquired with upright fluorescence microscopy (Nikon Eclipse C1), and the fluorescence intensity was quantitatively analyzed in Image J.

A DMEM medium with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin was used to cultivate the macrophage cell line RAW264.7. The material leaching solution was obtained according to our previously published literature [41]. RAW 264.7 cells were seeded in six-well plates (1 ×106 per well). After the cells were completely adherent, the leaching solution of ZE, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE and 100 ng/mL LPS, respectively, was added to the six-well plates corresponding to ZE group, TCDs@ZE group, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group, ZE + LPS group, TCDs@ZE + LPS group, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE + LPS group. After continous incubation 24 h, total RNA was extracted with the EZ-press RNA purification kit (EZbioscience, China). The mRNA expression was quantitatively detected with a Quantum Studio 7 instrument (Thermofisher, USA) after reverse transcription. All primers were synthesized by BioTNT with Table 1.

All data were presented quantitatively using Origin and GraphPad Prism software. For two independent samples, the F-test was applied to analyze the homogeneity of variance followed by t-test or the corrected t-test. For the analysis of differences among multiple groups of independent samples, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was applied first to analyze whether there were differences between groups and subsequently Tukey's post hoc test (homogeneous square difference) or Dunnett's T3 post hoc test (uneven variance) was performed to analyze the differences between two groups according to the homogeneity of variance. All p-value analyses were two-way, and p < 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and design strategy of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation and design strategy of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE are presented in Fig. 1. The prepared TCDs and CaO2 were added to pure zein. According to available information, zein will dissolve in aqueous ethanol solution if the ethanol content ranges over 60–95% [42]. The added CaO2 will react with the water in the solution, such that the ethanol concentration will increase, thereby reducing the solubility of zein. Using this characteristic, zein will deposit around CaO2, coating it with a protective layer to delay the reaction between CaO2 and water [43]. Subsequently, ethyl cellulose was added to increase the degree of crosslinking of the macromolecular chains in the solution. The primary mechanism is hydrogen bond formation between the hydroxyl group of ethyl cellulose and the amino group of zein, which reduces the free hydrophilic groups on the molecular chain, thus improving the water stability of the electrospun fiber membrane [44]. Finally, the various active factors released by TCDs/CaO2 @ZE play an antibacterial and healing role in wound treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacture of the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE wound dressing
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of CaO2, ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE are shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 2a. The shape of the nano-CaO2 was spherical, with as diameter of approximately 15–25 nm. The ZE composite fiber had a smooth surface and uniform structure without any beads. Following doping with CaO2 NPs, the fiber surface was transformed from smooth to rough and the bead structure was uniformly distributed in the fiber, suggesting that the expected core-shell structure had formed.

XRD patterns of CaO2 and the TCDs/CaO2 film are shown in Fig. 2b and c. Three main peaks were clearly observed at 2θ = 30.1, 35.6 and 47.3 (JCPDS card number 00–003–0865) [45], which corresponded to the (002), (110) and (112) crystal planes of CaO2 respectively, indicating that the structure of CaO2 encapsulated in nanofibers remained intact.

The ultraviolet-visible-near-infrared (UV–vis–NIR) spectrum revealed that CaO2 powders had a sharp absorption edge at approximately 345 nm and a wide absorption peak at approximately 400–600 nm (Fig. 2d), which indicated that it displayed a certain degree of absorption of visible light, but this was weak. However, the addition of TCDs appeared to have little effect on the optical properties of the films (Fig. 2e).

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra revealed that ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE displayed the same peak shape, position (3295 cm−1) and intensity of absorbance bands attributed to -NH and -OH groups of amino acid (Fig. 2f). FTIR spectra showed a lower intensity of the peak between 2930 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1, suggesting that there were strong hydrogen bond interactions between ethyl cellulose and zein [46], and the structural stability of the ZE fiber was unaffected by the addition of CaO2 or TCDs.

The thermal stability is shown in Fig. 2g. The ZE fibers were stable at 40 °C and started major mass loss at 300 °C. Thermal degradation of TCDs@ZE started at the same temperature and displayed a more gradual mass loss in the temperature range of 300–600 °C. The improvement of the thermal stability of the fiber membrane after adding TCDs was attributed to the presence of a large number of aromatic structures in the TCDs, which acted as a thermal barrier to prevent degradation [20]. It also showed that the TCDs was successfully loaded on ZE. Overall, the addition of TCDs and CaO2 had little effect on the thermal stability of ZE [47].

The mechanical characteristics were determined by the ASTM D683-Type V method, and measurements were conducted in triplicate (Table S1). The ethyl cellulose incorporation considerably improved the tensile strength up to 5.42 MPa, which was due to the physical interactions and hydrogen bonds between the zein chains and ethyl cellulose [46]. The mechanical properties of the fiber membrane decreased slightly with the addition of CaO2 and TCDs, which may be due to the excessive CaO2 and TCD particles acting as defects in the polymer matrix, resulting in a reduced tensile strength and modulus.

Due to its hydrophobic nature, zein is widely used in food storage and drug packaging, and the hydrophobic shell formed by its coating is expected to possess appreciable swelling and moisture resistance properties [43]. From Fig. 2h, ZE displayed little sign of swelling even after 48 h, indicating superior water resistance contributed by the addition of ethyl cellulose. The addition of TCDs had little effect on the swelling rate, while the addition of CaO2 decreased the swelling rate of the fiber membrane. It was hypothesized that the formation of a core-shell structure changed the original morphology of the fiber, which reduced the water absorption of the membrane and further reduced the swelling capacity [48].

The results of the water contact angle also confirmed that the appropriate addition of ethyl cellulose could improve the hydrophobicity and water resistance of zein electrospun nanofibers. By contrast, the addition of CaO2 NPs or TCDs had no noticeable effect on the water stability of the fiber membrane. This result showed that the CaO2 encapsulated in the fiber membrane can be protected from excessive reaction with water.

Fig. 2j shows the changes in the oxygen release rate of CaO2 and dressings containing various additives in an in vitro simulated environment. Neutrophils typically consume large amounts of oxygen during the inflammatory phase of wound healing [49]. Persistent hypoxia inhibits the release of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), whereas persistent oxygen overload produces large amounts of ROS, thereby resulting in persistent inflammation [50]. CaO2 and dressings containing CaO2 rapidly increased the content of dissolved oxygen in the system in the short term; the content then gradually decreased to the initial level and remained stable, thus indicating that the addition of CaO2 alleviated the hypoxic microenvironment of the wound in the initial stage of healing, accelerated the onset of wound healing [15], and maintained the oxygen concentration at normal levels without negatively affecting the subsequent healing stage. However, the dissolved oxygen content of dressings without CaO2 showed a continuous downward trend in the fluctuation range. This phenomenon might have been due to the adsorption of the fiber structure and the escape of oxygen from the surface of the solution.

Fig. 2k shows the release of calcium ions by CaO2 and dressings containing CaO2. Experimental results confirmed that the zein coating had significant effects in controlling the release and reaction of CaO2. The cumulative reaction amount of CaO2 in CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE within 24 h was less than 60%, whereas that of pure CaO2 exceeded 80%. CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE gradually released the remaining CaO2 in the subsequent 96 h, and the release curves were highly similar. The final cumulative release exceeded 90%. The mechanism of release of nanofibers may be that fiber swelling destroys the internal core-shell structure, thereby causing CaO2 to be released and reacted.

Because of the need for prolonged direct contact with open wounds, biocompatibility assessment is an important factor that must be considered in dressing design. Mouse fibroblasts (L929) were used to assess the cytocompatibility of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE. The ZE control group did not show obvious cytotoxicity, thus confirming zein’s good biocompatibility. The results in other groups confirmed that the addition of appropriate amounts of TCDs and CaO2 to the membrane did not significantly affect cell viability, presumably because the zein coating prevented the burst release of CaO2, thereby greatly decreasing the overall cytotoxicity of the dressing. Commercially available silver-containing dressings such as Acticoat*Absorbent had a loss rate of 69% of diabetic fibroblasts after 3 days of incubation [51]. Under the mitigating effect of TCDs, the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group showed better promotion of fibroblast growth than the other groups, indicating that the addition of TCDs further improved the biocompatibility of dressings.

The antibacterial activities of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films were investigated against S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli and P. aeruginosa ( Fig. 3). Filter paper disks infiltrated with ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films were prepared, and an agar disk diffusion test was conducted to test the susceptibility of the above bacteria to these materials.

As shown in Fig. 3b and Table S2, inhibition haloes were clearly observed around the paper disks infiltrated with CaO2 @ZE or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films, with the inhibition zone diameters of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE being more extensive than those of CaO2 @ZE in all groups, particularly in the S. aureus group, thus suggesting that the mitigating effect of TCDs does not act on bacteria but enhances the bacteriostatic performance of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE membranes, and the inhibitory effect of TCDs on S. aureus was as specific as that of tea polyphenols [52]. According to the results of other publications [53], it is hypothesized that the antibacterial mechanism of TCDs under non-light conditions is that small NPs are more readily ingested by bacteria and interact with macromolecules in the intracellular environment. The resultant overload of internal ROS exacerbates oxidative stress and damage to nucleic acids or proteins, which could result in the death of the bacteria. In addition to the antibacterial properties of TCDs, the synergistic relationship between TCDs and CaO2 in the antibacterial progress may also include a Fenton-like reaction between TCDs and H2O2 produced by CaO2, thus yielding more hydroxyl radicals that attack bacteria and achieving better antibacterial performance.

The ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE films diluted with Mueller Hinton broth were added to the culture medium of the tested bacteria to obtain their respective MIC and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC). TCDs/CaO2 @ZE with the lowest MIC and MBC values further confirmed a more effective antibacterial activity (Table S3). For the phenomenon that different strains had different bacteriostatic and bactericidal effects, it was hypothesized that differences in the tolerance to H2O2 was the main reason.

Additionally, the antibacterial rates of different strains were determined. Compared with the ZE membrane, the inhibition rates of TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE on E. coli were 7.10%, 81.88% and 93.75%, respectively, and the antibacterial rates against S. aureus were 10.10%, 86.25% and 98.83%, respectively (Fig. 3c). Therefore, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE has good broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against E. coli (gram-negative bacteria) and S. aureus (gram-positive bacteria).

A biofilm is a community of bacteria embedded in self-secreted extracellular matrix, and biofilm disruption is considered to be an evaluation of antibacterial activity. A crystal violet staining assay was conducted to compare the effects of ZE, TCDs@ZE, CaO2 @ZE and TCDs/CaO2 @ZE on biofilm formation and survival based on the optical density (OD) values at 570 nm. The results indicated that treatment with TCDs/CaO2 @ZE or CaO2 @ZE at the early stage markedly inhibited biofilm formation by S. aureus and E. coli, and the addition of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE or CaO2 @ZE significantly destroyed the biofilm that had been formed at the later phase (Fig. 3d). TCDs/CaO2 had a better inhibitory effect on the biofilm, further confirming the central role of CaO2 and the synergistic effect of TCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alamar blue assay and ROS measurement
ce:section.ce:para: As a cell activity indicator, Alamar Blue has become a standard method to evaluate pathogen sensitivity to antibacterial compounds. Compared with the other groups, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE had the lowest relative fluorescence intensity of reducing Alamar Blue (indicating living cells) (Fig. 3e). To further investigate the antibacterial mechanism of the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE membrane, NAC was added to each group of materials as a ROS inhibitor, and the ROS production level was determined using DCFH. The results showed that in the absence of NAC, a large amount of ROS was detected in the liquid medium of the CaO2 and TCDs/CaO2 groups, which was attributed to H2O2 produced by the reaction of CaO2 and H2O. After adding NAC, the ROS of these two groups decreased to the same level as that of the other groups. To confirm that ROS induced by the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE film played a significant role in the antibacterial process, the Alamar Blue test was applied again to determine the antibacterial effect of the TCDs/CaO2 group after adding NAC. The results showed no remaining antibacterial ability of the TCDs/CaO2 group after excluding the influence of NAC itself, indicating that ROS produced by CaO2 played a dominant role in the antibacterial process. It is of note that the test results for E. coli were slightly different from those of the other strains. Compared with the CaO2 group, the ROS of E. coli in the TCDs/CaO2 group was significantly increased (Fig. 3f), and after the ROS was eliminated by NAC, the TCDs/CaO2 group still retained some antibacterial ability against E. coli (Fig. 3e). These results may indicate that the synergy of CaO2 and TCDs has some specificity in the elimination of E. coli, similarly as of S. aureus.

We evaluated the effect of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE on the repair of full-thickness excised wounds in STZ-induced diabetic rats. To determine the effect of CaO2 and TCDs release on wound healing, ZE without TCDs or CaO2 was used as the control group. The digital photos and quantification of the closure rate of the wound on the third, sixth and tenth days after trauma are presented in Fig. 4a and b, showing that the skin gradually regenerated from the edge of the wound. Compared with the control and TCDs@ZE groups, the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group significantly accelerated the wound closure rate at each time point, showing its positive impact on wound healing. It was also evident that CaO2 and TCDs had more pronounced effects during.

the early stage of wound healing through the quantitative results of the wound healing rate. On the third day of dressing treatment, the wound healing rate of the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group reached 43.6%, which was significantly higher than that of the control group (21.3%), and the 31.6% healing rate of the TCDs@ZE group also showed that TCDs played a vital role in the process of wound healing. On the sixth day, the healing rate of the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group reached 74.6%, with the difference greater still compared with the other two groups (TCDs@ZE: 58.3% and control: 46.6%). On the tenth day, the wound healing rate of rats in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group was 90.6%, and the newly formed skin had grown thick hair without prominent scarring. These data showed that both CaO2 and TCDs effectively promoted wound healing in STZ-induced diabetic rats. The excellent performance of the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group in the early stages of wound healing might have been because the produced hydrogen peroxide induced macrophages to recruit leukocytes in an early stage, thereby accelerating the initiation of wound healing [54].
To evaluate the healing effect of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE from the perspective of histology, H&E and Masson trichrome staining were performed on regenerated skin tissue collected on day 10.

The histological results were quantified, and the wound closure was evaluated by determining the mean width of the open wound. As shown in the Fig. 4c, after 10 days of treatment, scab tissue partially wrapped with dead inflammatory cells was still observed in the control group, while no obvious scab was found in the TCDs@ZE or TCDs/CaO2 @ZE groups. By comparing the quantitative results of wound closure in each group (Fig. 4d), the width of the unclosed wound decreased in the order of the ZE group, the TCDs@ZE group and the TCDs/CaO2 group, which confirmed the above results of the wound healing rate. These results indicated that the addition of CaO2 and TCDs to the dressing was beneficial in alleviating the inflammatory environment caused by wound healing, thus promoting the regeneration of wound epithelial tissue. In addition, it was observed that the fibroblasts in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE and TCDs@ZE groups had a high proliferation behavior, as well as accelerating the production of sebaceous glands and hair follicles. In particular, the integrity of the repaired epidermis in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group was greater, while the degree of control group remained at a low level.

In the process of wound healing, collagen synthesized by fibroblasts is the main component of extracellular matrix. The presence of collagen at the wound site is crucial for the final repair of the wound. As shown in Fig. 4e, collagen deposition in the control group was mainly distributed in the dermis below the epidermis. Collagen deposition in the TCDs@ZE group was more pronounced in the upper dermis and the wound defect compared to the control group, whereas in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group, collagen deposition was uniform and evident in all parts of the epidermis and dermis. Further epidermal proliferation and angiogenesis was also observed in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group by pairwise comparison between groups. Therefore, although collagen deposition occurred in all three groups, the wound healing in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group was the best according to the collagen distribution and morphology observed in Fig. 4f. We believe that this finding was because the release of CaO2 in the dressing increased the concentration of Ca2 + around the wound, and Ca2+ positively influenced the secretion of numerous cytokines such as VEGF and fibroblast growth factor (FGF), thereby playing a positive role in wound healing through promoting the regeneration of vascular and granulation tissue [55,56]. The properties inherited by TCDs from tea polyphenols may also be a potential mechanism for promoting wound healing. Finally, in the stage of wound remodeling, epidermal hyperplasia decreased, and dermal collagen reorganization was observed with TCDs/CaO2 @ZE treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulation effect on the inflammatory microenvironment
ce:section.ce:para: Regulation of the expression levels of inflammatory factors in the wound microenvironment plays a crucial role in wound healing and tissue regeneration, and the persistent inflammatory phase of chronic wounds can hinder cell proliferation and vascularization, thus affecting the normal recovery of wounds. Inhibition of the overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines effectively improves the speed and quality of wound healing. To further elucidate the anti-inflammatory mechanism of CaO2 and TCDs, quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction was performed to analyze the effects of CaO2 and TCDs on the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) under in vitro conditions. The results showed that the active expression of pro-inflammatory factors induced by LPS can be effectively inhibited by TCDs and CaO2. It is of note that the TCDs@ZE group displayed a significant blocking behavior of pro-inflammatory factors, indicating that TCDs have an excellent effect in the process of inhibiting inflammation. On the basis of this, adding CaO2 can further enhance the dressing's ability to inhibit the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, thereby alleviating the inflammatory response caused by macrophages.

On this basis, after 10 days of wound treatment with different materials, rat wound peripheral tissues were collected, and immunofluorescence staining of the macrophage M1 inflammatory phenotypic marker CCR7 and the M2 repair phenotypic marker CD206 was performed to evaluate the transformation effect of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE on macrophages in vivo. As shown in Fig. 4H, the fluorescence intensity of the M2 phenotypic marker in the ZE group was weak, thus indicating that most macrophages still differentiated toward the M1 type. After treatment with TCDs/CaO2 @ZE, the proportion of macrophages that differentiated toward the M2 type in tissues significantly increased, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the M2 phenotypic marker in the TCDs@ZE group was slightly lower than that in the TCDs/CaO2 @ZE group. In conclusion, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE dressings promote the transformation of macrophages from M1 to M2 in diabetic rat wound models, shorten the duration of the inflammatory stage, and facilitate further wound healing. Fig. 5.

In this study, we used ethyl cellulose-modified zein as the substrate of membrane, added nano-calcium peroxide and TCDs to co-spin silk and prepared a film dressing with antibacterial and healing properties. Due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonding and its own fiber structure, the dressing displayed good mechanical properties and histocompatibility. The formed core-shell structure reduced the contact between CaO2 NPs and water to reduce the reaction rate. Under the combined action of TCDs and CaO2, TCDs/CaO2 @ZE displayed good biocompatibility, which effectively inhibited the growth and colonization of bacteria in terms of antibacterial action and had significant inhibitory and killing effects on E. coli (gram-negative bacteria) and S. aureus (gram-positive bacteria). The results of the in vivo experiments showed that the addition of TCDs and CaO2 accelerated the healing rate of wounds and reduced the length of scars. The use of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE had a positive effect on fibroblast proliferation, collagen deposition, re-epithelialization and inhibition of proinflammatory factors. The experimental results of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE showed that it can significantly improve the wound healing efficiency of chronic diabetes wounds, and we believe that the synthetic material and process of TCDs/CaO2 @ZE generation conform to the concept of green, environmental protection and simplicity, indicating its potential as an advanced treatment dressing. The synergistic effect of TCDs and CaO2 also provides a new and effective treatment method for chronic wound management.

Zhenyou Dong and Junhui Yin were involved in the Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis and writing of the original draft. Xueqing Zhou, Suyun Li, Zhenyu Fu and Pei Liu were involved in the Investigation and performed the research. Longxiang Shen and Wenyan Shi helped in Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, and Writing – review & editing.
